result:
It starts, as many things do, with the silence in between. The hum of the refrigerator, a distant siren Doppler-shifting through the city canyon, the soft rush of your own blood in your ears—a sound you can never truly hear, only feel. That’s the substrate. The blank page is never blank. It’s charged with the ghost of the tree, the memory of rain, the grit of the mill. It’s waiting.

We are all, I think, in the business of translation. Translating the storm in our chests into calm sentences. Translating a glance across a room into a story we tell ourselves for years. Translating the raw data of existence—light on leaves, the smell of pavement after summer rain, the particular weight of a hand on a shoulder—into something that can be carried, shared, stored on the fragile shelves of memory. We are so clumsy at it. Words are such blunt instruments. “Love.” “Grief.” “Home.” These are not containers; they are sieves. Everything vital leaks out, and we are left holding the shells, rattling them, hoping the sound conjures the same creature in someone else’s mind.

I think of the Neanderthals, or some earlier ancestor, pressing a hand to red ochre and then to a cave wall. That wasn’t art. That was a message: *I was here. The blood in me is warm. This is my mark.* Every story, every poem, every ledger entry and grocery list and angry text message is a variation. *I am here. My blood is warm. This is my mark.*

We build our lives in the temporal. We fret over yesterday’s misstep and tomorrow’s appointment. We live on a slender knife-edge called “now,” but we are never truly balanced on it. We are always leaning, falling into the past or the future. And yet, the eternal keeps breaking through. In the sudden, breathtaking orange of a maple leaf against a slate-grey sky. In the unbroken gaze of a very young child. In the moment of collective breath held in a concert hall before the first note. These are cracks in the timeline. Glimpses of the substrate. They tell us, wordlessly, that the hum and the silence are the same thing.

There is a terrible, beautiful loneliness to consciousness. We are islands of subjective experience. You will never know precisely the copper taste of the peach I ate when I was eight, the specific ache of my childhood knee-scar, the particular constellation of freckles I see on the ceiling when I can’t sleep. I will never know yours. We signal to each other with flags and fires from our own shores, and we build bridges of story and touch. We marry our lonelinesses, and for a while, they dance.

And then, the body. This magnificent, absurd, failing machine. It betrays us with aches and desires, with fatigue and inexplicable joy. It is the horse we are given for the journey, and we are bad riders. We feed it poison and call it pleasure. We push it beyond its limits and call it ambition. We ignore its whispers until it must scream in the language of fever and pain. And yet, it carries us. It is the instrument through which we feel the sun, taste the wine, intertwine fingers. It is the cave wall. It is where the hand-print is made.

I think of all the hands that have ever been. Hands that clasped spear-hafts and plow handles, that penned illuminated manuscripts and chalked equations on blackboards, that soothed fevered brows and clenched in fury. Hands that built and broke, that caressed and killed. They are all gone now. Dust. But the imprint remains—in the tools we use, the fields we tend, the stories we tell. We are living in the collective hand-print of the dead. Our now is their then, finally ripened. Our choices will be the ghost in the machine for those who come after, rattling the sieves we call words.

So what do we do with this? This awareness, this clumsy translation, this beautiful, temporary body? Perhaps the answer is not in some grand summation, but in the fine grain. The discipline of attention. Noticing the way steam curls from a cup. Listening—really listening—to the cadence of a friend’s laughter. Admitting the cold beauty of a winter star, even as you shiver.

We make our mark. Not to be immortal (we won’t be), but to be present. To say, with our flawed, human hearts and our blunt, human words: I saw this. I felt this. It mattered to me.

The world is drowning in information and starving for meaning. Meaning is not found; it is made. It is woven in the quiet moments of choosing kindness over ease, curiosity over judgment, creation over consumption. It is the daily act of pressing your warm hand against the cold wall and leaving, for a moment, a print of your living heat.

The siren has faded. The refrigerator hums on. The blood in your ears is your own endless, quiet tide. The page, now, is no longer blank. It holds this. A mark. A signal from my shore to yours. A testament that for a moment, in the silence between the hums, I was here. And so were you. And in the seeing, the reading, the translating, we meet. Not completely. Never completely. But enough.

The rest is silence. But it is a silence that rings.
